safety_signals.meaning: increased combat capability and survival potential

industrial/corporate building environments representing corporate control

world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: RoboCop advances forward, engaging hostiles; stage structure reflects film narrative

world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: enemies actively threaten player; combat required for progression

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: bonus screen shifts to first-person target shooting range perspective

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: intermission sequences using digitized voice samples from film actors

environmental_storytelling.example: stages directly adapted from 1987 RoboCop film establish world context and criminal jurisdiction
OCP corporate facilities and urban crime sectors visually differentiate organizational control zones
transition to first-person shooting range communicates skill assessment within law enforcement protocol
digitized voice acting from film actors provides diegetic story progression without cutscenes

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: advances through stages, engages hostiles with weapons, completes law enforcement objectives
distribute across stage environment, oppose player progression, require combat engagement
transitions to first-person perspective for target acquisition and precision shooting

notes: RoboCop (NES) is a run-and-gun/beat-em-up hybrid action game. Primary visual communication relies on sprite-based enemy placement, stage progression tied to film narrative, and gameplay mode shifts (side-view combat vs. first-person shooting) to convey world-state changes. Faction identity is communicated through environmental context (criminal zones vs. corporate facilities) rather than explicit visual markers. The game lacks detailed political/systemic communication—danger is purely threat-based (enemies present) rather than systemic (corruption, corporate control manifested visually). Source material is limited to overview data; specific NES visual asset documentation unavailable.
